summary: This anonymous guest post explains the controversy surrounding tenure changes at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. The state is moving tenure authority from state law to the politically-appointed Board of Regents, while adding vague language about 'program redirection' that could allow termination of tenured faculty. Combined with weakened shared governance that makes faculty subordinate to chancellors on program decisions, the worst-case scenario would give politically-appointed administrators unilateral power to eliminate tenure protections. The author, writing anonymously to demonstrate why tenure matters, warns that this could happen at any university.
key_points: Tenure authority is moving from state law to the Board of Regents, who are effectively political appointees of the Governor
New language about 'program redirection' expands grounds for terminating tenured faculty beyond the traditional standard of financial exigency
Shared governance is being weakened, making faculty subordinate to chancellors on program and curriculum decisions
The combination of these changes could allow politically-motivated termination of faculty, which is precisely what tenure is designed to prevent
The author's anonymity itself demonstrates the chilling effect that weakened tenure protections create
money_quotes: I choose to do so anonymously, in part to make a point about the value of tenure and the protection it affords faculty from becoming political targets.
Other than dismissal for cause, most schools only allow for termination of tenured faculty for reasons of financial exigency.
The Regents are appointed by the Governor, with the most recent appointee being the son of a Bradley Foundation member.
Add these things up, and the Regents could appoint a Chancellor with the authority to unilaterally make program changes and terminate tenured faculty.
If someone's house was on fire, would you grab a bucket of water, or would you think about stealing their TV?
Make no mistake that your university could be next, so do onto others as you would have them do onto you.
key_insight: The real threat to academic freedom isn't the formal elimination of tenure but the transfer of its definition to political appointees who can rewrite its protections at will.
